verses.text: 'Man that is born of a woman is of few days, and full of trouble,' fading like a flower and a shadow. Job mourns the frailty of human life. To number our days rightly is to learn how small and brief we are before the eternal God.

'Man that is born of a woman is of few days, and full of trouble,' fading like a flower and a shadow. Job mourns the frailty of human life. To number our days rightly is to learn how small and brief we are before the eternal God.

'Man that is born of a woman is of few days, and full of trouble,' fading like a flower and a shadow. Job mourns the frailty of human life. To number our days rightly is to learn how small and brief we are before the eternal God.

Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Our days are fixed; let God turn away and give us a little rest. Job owns the taint we are born with. None can cleanse himself; only God can make the unclean clean.

Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Our days are fixed; let God turn away and give us a little rest. Job owns the taint we are born with. None can cleanse himself; only God can make the unclean clean.

Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Our days are fixed; let God turn away and give us a little rest. Job owns the taint we are born with. None can cleanse himself; only God can make the unclean clean.

There is hope for a tree, he says: cut down, it may sprout again at the scent of water. Nature seems to promise renewal. The longing for life beyond the axe stirs in him, though he cannot yet see how.

There is hope for a tree, he says: cut down, it may sprout again at the scent of water. Nature seems to promise renewal. The longing for life beyond the axe stirs in him, though he cannot yet see how.

There is hope for a tree, he says: cut down, it may sprout again at the scent of water. Nature seems to promise renewal. The longing for life beyond the axe stirs in him, though he cannot yet see how.

But man dies, wastes away, and lies down, not to wake 'till the heavens be no more.' Death looks to him final and dark. Without the light of resurrection, the grave seems an end from which none return.

But man dies, wastes away, and lies down, not to wake 'till the heavens be no more.' Death looks to him final and dark. Without the light of resurrection, the grave seems an end from which none return.

But man dies, wastes away, and lies down, not to wake 'till the heavens be no more.' Death looks to him final and dark. Without the light of resurrection, the grave seems an end from which none return.

Then hope flickers: 'O that thou wouldest hide me in the grave... till thy wrath be past,' for God would call and he would answer. He dreams of being kept till the storm passes. Faith reaches, however dimly, toward a life on the far side of death.

Then hope flickers: 'O that thou wouldest hide me in the grave... till thy wrath be past,' for God would call and he would answer. He dreams of being kept till the storm passes. Faith reaches, however dimly, toward a life on the far side of death.

Then hope flickers: 'O that thou wouldest hide me in the grave... till thy wrath be past,' for God would call and he would answer. He dreams of being kept till the storm passes. Faith reaches, however dimly, toward a life on the far side of death.
Yet despair returns: God numbers his steps and seals up his transgression in a bag. He feels his every fault watched and stored. The sense of unforgiven sin makes the soul shrink under God's eye.
Yet despair returns: God numbers his steps and seals up his transgression in a bag. He feels his every fault watched and stored. The sense of unforgiven sin makes the soul shrink under God's eye.

As mountains crumble and waters wear away stones, so, he says, God destroys the hope of man. Time and trouble grind all things down. Earthly hope is as perishable as the rocks, if it has no anchor beyond this life.

As mountains crumble and waters wear away stones, so, he says, God destroys the hope of man. Time and trouble grind all things down. Earthly hope is as perishable as the rocks, if it has no anchor beyond this life.

As mountains crumble and waters wear away stones, so, he says, God destroys the hope of man. Time and trouble grind all things down. Earthly hope is as perishable as the rocks, if it has no anchor beyond this life.

A man's sons may rise to honour or sink low, and he knows it not; only his own flesh and soul feel pain. Death cuts us off from all we leave behind. The dust of this life cannot follow us; we go to God alone.
